title: Problem 1 (Alon [1], Problem 4.1)
statement_text: Does there exist an absolute constant $c > 0$ such that the following holds for all pairs of natural numbers $(n, s)$? Every $2n$-vertex digraph $D$ of minimum out-degree at least $s$ contains an $n$-vertex subdigraph of minimum out-degree at least $\frac{s}{2} - c$.
context_text: Alon raised this problem in 2006 in the context of digraph splitting. He proved a weaker bound: every $2n$-vertex digraph of minimum out-degree at least $s$ contains an $n$-vertex subdigraph of minimum out-degree at least $\frac{s}{2} - O(\sqrt{s\log s})$. The current paper resolves the problem in the negative.
paper_contribution: Resolves the problem in the negative: for infinitely many $n$ there exists a $2n$-vertex tournament with minimum out-degree $s = n-1$ in which every $n$-vertex subdigraph has minimum out-degree at most $\frac{s}{2} - \left(\frac{1}{2}+o(1)\right)\log_3(s)$, so no absolute constant $c$ can work.
notes: PDF source — some inline math fragments were garbled; statement reconstructed from both the abstract and the body of Problem 1.
paper_title: Subdigraphs of prescribed size and outdegree

title: Open problem: closing the gap for $d(s)$
statement_text: It remains an interesting open problem to close the gap between the lower and upper bounds: $\Omega(\log s) < \frac{s}{2} - d(s) < O(\sqrt{s\log s})$, where $d(s)$ denotes the largest integer such that every $2n$-vertex digraph of minimum out-degree at least $s$ contains an $n$-vertex subdigraph of minimum out-degree at least $d(s)$.
context_text: After disproving Alon's problem, the authors note that the true asymptotic behaviour of $\frac{s}{2} - d(s)$ lies somewhere between $\Omega(\log s)$ (established by their construction) and $O(\sqrt{s\log s})$ (established by Alon's probabilistic argument). The precise asymptotics of $d(s)$ remain open.
notes: Stated in prose without a labelled environment; PDF source may garble some exponent notation.
paper_title: Subdigraphs of prescribed size and outdegree
